name: Hopkins Grammar School
notes:
Founded by bequest of Edward Hopkins, Governor of Connecticut and overseer of Thomas Hooker's will. Hopkins also named Mary Newton (H00003) and Sarah Wilson (H00006) in his own will with direct bequests.
[founder] Edward Hopkins

name: Harvard College
notes: Rev. Thomas Shepard (I00002) was instrumental in founding Harvard and placing it at Cambridge. Rev. Samuel Shepard (H00008) graduated 1658; Samuel Shepard grandson (H00038) graduated 1685; Col. Roger Newton (H00044) also a graduate.

name: First Church of Christ, Hartford
notes: Traces roots to village of Little Baddow, Essex County, England. Moved to current spot adjacent Ancient Burying Ground in 1739.

name: Mendon Congregational Church
notes: Rev. Grindal Rawson served as pastor approximately 33 years.

name: First Church of Christ, Farmington
notes: Roger Newton first pastor 1645-1657; Samuel Hooker second pastor 1661-1697. Seven Pillars at founding: Rev. Roger Newton, Stephen Hart, John Bronson, John Cole, Thomas Thompson, Thomas Judd, Robert Porter.

name: Emmanuel College, Cambridge
notes: Founded by Sir Walter Mildmay. Thomas Hooker entered 1604, B.A. 1608, M.A. 1611, Fellow 1611-c.1618.

name: Market Bosworth Grammar School
notes: Thomas Hooker attended c. 1595-1604; approximately 10 miles from Tilton. Shuffelton's claim rests on Dixie fellowship restriction documented in Venn & Venn Alumni Cantabrigienses (1922).

name: First Church of Milford
notes: Roger Newton second pastor 1660-1683, succeeding Rev. Peter Prudden; installed Aug 22, 1660. Abraham Pierson, first President of Yale College, was trained in Newton's home here.

name: Wesleyan University
notes: Wesleyan University in Middletown CT (founded 1831). The Samuel Wadsworth Russell House (High Street Middletown, 1828, architect Ithiel Town) is now the Wesleyan Honors College — built by Thomas Hooker descendant Samuel Wadsworth Russell (1789-1862), founder of Russell & Company in China. Several Hooker-descendant Russell family homes along High Street Middletown are now part of the Wesleyan campus.

name: New Haven Law School
notes: The New Haven Law School, affiliated with Yale. Edwards Pierrepont graduated 1840. Now integrated as Yale Law School.

primary_name: The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints
name: The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints
notes: Newton Tuttle (1825-1905), great-grandson of Giles Pierpont (H00372) and great-great-grandson of Lt. Joseph Pierpont and Hannah Russell Pierpont, converted to the LDS Church and emigrated to Utah 1854 with the William Field Company. His diary documenting the overland journey is held in the LDS Church archives, Salt Lake City. One of the more unexpected genealogical destinations of the Hooker-Pierpont line.

name: Palm Springs Art Museum
notes: The Palm Springs Art Museum, originally the Palm Springs Desert Museum, was founded in part through the land gift of Cornelia Butler White (1874-1961), a Thomas Hooker descendant through the Brooklyn Pierrepont branch. In 1947, Cornelia White deeded the property along Tahquitz Canyon Way between Palm Canyon Drive and Indian Canyon Drive to the museum. The main-floor gallery is dedicated to Cornelia White. "She requested no publicity and only retained a life tenancy on a portion." The railroad-tie house built by Welwood Murray on the property was moved in 1979 to the Village Green on South Palm Canyon Drive, where it remains today. Thomas Hooker → Samuel Hooker → Mary Hooker Pierpont (H00034) → Hezekiah Pierpont (HD0419) → John Pierpont (H00388) → Hezekiah Beers Pierrepont → William Constable Pierrepont → Anna Maria Pierrepont White → Cornelia Butler White.

name: Connecticut Historical Society
notes: The Connecticut Historical Society (Hartford CT) holds multiple objects connected to Hooker family descendants. Most notably: the HOOKER-PIERPONT-RUSSELL-TALCOTT FAMILY APRON — a 1730s silk embroidery on Chinese taffeta with French metallic thread, made by or for one of three "Mary" generations: Mary Hooker Pierpont (H00034, 1673-1740), Mary Pierpont Russell (H00118, 1702-1740), or Mary Russell Talcott (daughter of H00118, 1720-1799). Researched by Olivia R. Scott for her Master's thesis; presented at the Decorative Arts Trust 7th Annual Emerging Scholars Colloquium, Park Avenue Armory, New York City, January 22, 2023. Video: "Genealogical Threads: Unraveling the History of the Hooker-Pierpont-Russell-Talcott Family Apron." Scott's research found Chinese silk (not English), French metallic threads, and European-dyed embroidery silk — and argues the most likely embroiderer was Mary Russell Talcott (married 1740). ALSO: The Connecticut Historical Society holds an unfinished needlework piece with unused sticks of embroidery silk wrapped in paper, associated with the same family.
research_contact: Olivia R. Scott (Master's thesis, Decorative Arts Trust grant)

name: American Geographical Society
notes: Founded 1851 by 31 New Yorkers, originally to organize a search for the lost Franklin Polar Expedition. Among the founders: Henry Evelyn Pierrepont (HD1533, gen 6) and Rev. Joshua Leavitt (HD0867, gen 6) — two Hooker descendants through entirely separate branches, almost certainly unaware of their shared ancestry. Pierrepont served on its Council 1852-1862. Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Geographical_Society

name: The Emancipator
notes: Abolitionist newspaper founded by Arthur Tappan 1833. Rev. Joshua Leavitt (HD0867) editor from 1836. One of the most influential abolitionist publications in antebellum America. Leavitt used it to circulate "Financial Power of Slavery" (1840), one of the most widely distributed abolitionist tracts of the era. Active through c.1850.

name: Deerfield Academy
notes: One of the oldest independent secondary schools in the United States. Rev. Claudius Herrick (X00500) was the second head of Deerfield Academy.

name: Rutgers College (now Rutgers University)
notes: Founded 1766 as Queen's College. Renamed Rutgers College 1825. Now Rutgers, The State University of New Jersey. Merrill Edwards Gates (HD1687) served as 9th President 1882-1890. During his tenure: built Winants Hall (first dormitory), New Jersey Hall, established Agricultural Experiment Station (Hatch Act 1887), expanded curriculum and library. Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rutgers_University

name: Amherst College
notes: Founded 1821. One of the oldest liberal arts colleges in the United States. Merrill Edwards Gates (HD1687) served as 6th President 1890-1899. Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Amherst_College David Peck Todd (HD1758) was astronomy professor and observatory director 1881-1917. Millicent Todd Bingham (HD1759) received honorary LL.D. 1957.

name: Albany Academy
notes: One of the oldest secondary schools in the United States. Merrill Edwards Gates (HD1687) served as principal for 12 years (c.1870-1882), growing enrollment from 70 to 800 students and extending the curriculum from 8 to 12 years. He declined the Chancellorship of the University of Tennessee (1875) to remain at Albany. Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Albany_Academy

name: University of Rochester
notes: Founded 1850. Merrill Edwards Gates (HD1687) graduated 1870 with honors in Mathematics, Latin, Greek, and the English Essay Prize. The university awarded him an honorary LL.D. in 1882. Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Rochester Asahel Clark Kendrick (I00943) was among the founding faculty (1850), served as inaugural Munro Professor of Greek 1850-1888, first librarian 1850-1866, acting president 1863 and 1877-78. His son Ryland Morris Kendrick (HD4668, class of 1889) taught Greek and Latin on the same faculty. Ryland's son Nathaniel Cooper Kendrick (HD4670) earned B.A. 1921 here before his Harvard doctorate. Nathaniel's daughter Ann Kendrick McNabb (HD4672) also received her B.A. in History here. Kendrick Hall, the first men's dormitory (1913), was named for Asahel.

name: U.S. Board of Indian Commissioners

sources.note: Merrill Edwards Gates; Board of Indian Commissioners

notes: Advisory body established 1869 to oversee federal Indian affairs and disbursement of funds. Abolished 1933. Merrill Edwards Gates (HD1687) served as Chairman 1889-1899 and Secretary 1899-1912 — 23 years total shaping federal Indian policy. He also chaired the Lake Mohonk Conferences on the Indian Question.

primary_name: Yale University (College of Connecticut / Yale College)
name: Yale University (College of Connecticut / Yale College)
notes:
Co-founded 1701 by a consortium of Congregational ministers including Rev. James Pierpont (H00116) — son of Mary Hooker Pierpont (H00034), daughter of Rev. Thomas Hooker. James Pierpont is a direct 2nd-generation Hooker descendant and one of Yale's primary founders. Yale's founding is inseparable from the Hooker-Pierpont family network. James Pierpont's daughter Sarah Pierpont (HD0418) married Rev. Jonathan Edwards; their grandson Aaron Burr Jr. (H00913) attended Princeton. The Yale connections in this database are tracked separately in the `yale_connections` array (95 entries as of v0.3.25), covering alumni, faculty, and other relationships across the Hooker tree. Primary Hooker connection: H00116 James Pierpont (co-founder), H00034 Mary Hooker Pierpont (his mother). WHITNEY PORTRAIT: The Morse portrait of Eli Whitney (1822) — accession 1827.1, among the earliest formally catalogued works — was donated by George Hoadley (B.A. 1801), husband of Hooker descendant Mary Ann Woolsey Hoadley (HD2075). Whitney had married Hooker descendant Henrietta Frances Edwards (HD2046). Yale Art Gallery: https://artgallery.yale.edu/collections/objects/31 PERCIVAL PAPERS: James Gates Percival (X01936, B.A. 1815, M.D. 1820) — papers held at Yale Beinecke Library (YCAL MSS 703), including correspondence with Emma Hart Willard (H00597) in which both Hooker descendants acknowledged their family connection. T02264 Dr. Alvan Talcott Jr. (Yale BA 1824, Yale MD 1831) endowed the Alvan Talcott Professor of Classics at Yale in 1888. Chair most recently named to Egbert Bakker (Oct 2016).
[hooker_connection] co-founded by direct Hooker descendant
[external Yale affiliations, no tree entry: EXTERNAL_DWIGHT, EXTERNAL_WHR, X01936 — detail in connections archive]

name: Princeton University (College of New Jersey)
notes:
Founded 1746 by Presbyterian New Side ministers including Rev. Aaron Burr Sr. (X00613) — who became its 2nd president, and who married Esther Edwards Burr (H00378), 4th-generation Hooker descendant. Rev. Jonathan Edwards (X00128) — father of H00378 and H00376, father-in-law of Aaron Burr Sr. — succeeded Burr Sr. as 3rd president of Princeton in 1758, dying there of smallpox inoculation complications 16 days before his daughter Esther died. NOTE: Jonathan Edwards is NOT himself a Hooker descendant — he married Sarah Pierpont (HD0418), who IS a Hooker descendant. Their children (H00376, H00378) are. Aaron Burr Jr. (H00913) — 5th-generation Hooker descendant — attended Princeton (admitted as sophomore age 13; B.A. 1772). Tapping Reeve (X00614) — founded America's first law school; married H00912 Sally Burr (Hooker descendant) — also Princeton graduate (B.A. 1763). Aaron Burr Reeve (HD1910) — Yale B.A., son of H00912. John Pierre Burr (HD1915) — Aaron Burr Jr.'s son; barred from attending Princeton because of his race, despite being grandson and great-grandson of two Princeton presidents (Burr Sr. and Edwards). The Princeton Slavery Project's essay on John Pierre Burr documents this irony in full.
[hooker_connection] president and students who are Hooker descendants or in-laws

name: Litchfield Law School
notes:
Founded 1784 by Judge Tapping Reeve (X00614) — the first law school in the United States. Tapping Reeve married Sarah "Sally" Burr Reeve (H00912), 5th-generation Hooker descendant, daughter of Esther Edwards Burr (H00378). Aaron Burr Jr. (H00913) — 5th-generation Hooker descendant — studied law here under his brother-in-law Tapping Reeve before enlisting in 1775. Among Litchfield's other notable students: John C. Calhoun (also became VP — two Vice Presidents produced by one law school). The school ran until 1833; at its peak enrolled students from across the country. Tapping Reeve House and Law School designated National Historic Landmark, 1965. In 1781, Reeve (with Theodore Sedgwick) represented Elizabeth Freeman ("Bett") in Brom & Bett v. Ashley — case that led to abolition of slavery in Massachusetts. Reeve also brought Rev. Lyman Beecher (father of Harriet Beecher Stowe) to Litchfield. The Litchfield Law School is among the most consequential institutions connected to the Hooker tree.
[hooker_connection] founded by in-law of Hooker descendant; Hooker descendant studied here

primary_name: Howard University / Freedman's Hospital School of Nursing
name: Howard University / Freedman's Hospital School of Nursing
notes:
Sara Louise Iredell Fleetwood (HD1935) — 8th-generation Hooker descendant and great-granddaughter of Aaron Burr Jr. (H00913) — enrolled in the first class admitted to Howard University's Freedman's Hospital School of Nursing in 1893, studying under Dr. Daniel Hale Williams (who had performed the first successful open-heart surgery the previous year). She graduated with Freedman's first graduating class in 1896 and in 1901 was appointed the first Black nursing superintendent at Freedman's Hospital, having outscored all national applicants on the civil service examination. Chief surgeon Dr. William A. Warfield gave her the title 'Directress of Nurses.' She organized the Freedmen's Nursing Association and served as the first Black woman on DC's nurse examining board (1907). Her husband Christian Fleetwood (Medal of Honor, Battle of Chaffin's Farm 1864) clerked at the Freedmen's Bureau after the Civil War. The Fleetwoods hosted weekly literary salons at 319 U Street NW — part of the African American Heritage Trail, Washington DC.
[hooker_connection] 8th-generation Hooker descendant served as first Black nursing superintendent

name: Philadelphia Female Anti-Slavery Society
notes:
Founded 1833 — among the earliest interracial abolitionist organizations in the US. Hester "Hetty" Elizabeth Emery Burr (X00621) — wife of John Pierre Burr (HD1915), 6th-generation Hooker descendant — was a member of the Society and participated in the Women's Anti-Slavery Conventions of 1838 and 1839. Charlotte Forten's paternal aunts (Margaretta Forten, Sarah Louisa Forten Purvis, Harriet Forten Purvis) and her grandmother Charlotte Vandine Forten were founding members of the Society. Charlotte Forten herself (connected to the Hooker tree through her aunt Annie Wood's marriage to John G. Webb HD1930) was deeply embedded in this network. Hetty Emery Burr also co-founded the Moral Reform Retreat — Philadelphia's first shelter for African American women. The Society connected the Hooker-Burr abolitionist network with the Forten-Purvis-Grimké abolitionist network that dominated Philadelphia Black civic life for three generations.
[hooker_connection] co-founded by wives and in-laws of Hooker tree members

name: Hamilton College
notes: Liberal arts college in Clinton, Oneida County NY. Sereno Edwards Dwight (HD1970) served as 3rd President 1833-1835 (his career cut short by mercury poisoning). Benjamin Woodbridge Dwight (HD1988) graduated 1835; was tutor 1839-1842. Theodore William Dwight (HD1989) graduated 1840; taught 1842-1858; Dean of Hamilton Law School 1853 before moving to Columbia. Elliott Anthony (husband of HD1991) studied law here under Theodore William Dwight. Benjamin Woolsey Dwight (HD1967) was treasurer of Hamilton College. Asahel Clark Kendrick (I00943) graduated A.B. 1831 and immediately joined the faculty as a tutor, beginning his career in classical languages before moving to Madison University (Colgate).

name: New York Law School
notes: Co-founded in 1891 by Theodore William Dwight (HD1989) after leading a faculty walkout from Columbia Law School in protest of trustees' attempt to convert to the Harvard case method. The Dwight method of instruction continues in modified form at NYLS today.

name: Chicago Law Institute
notes: Founded 1857 largely through the efforts of Judge Elliott Anthony (X00639, husband of HD1991 Mary Dwight Anthony, granddaughter of Yale president Timothy Dwight IV). Anthony drove the charter through Springfield and served as its officer for 15+ years. By 1871 the Institute held ~7,000 volumes of law books valued at ~$30,000, including a complete set of American and English reports, and many volumes from Chancellor Kent's library with his manuscript notes. GREAT CHICAGO FIRE Oct 8, 1871: The entire library was destroyed. "Smoke still rose from the ruins of the city" at the post-fire meeting, "and the deliberations were more than once interrupted by the falling of crumbling portions of the masonry of the Court-room." The Institute was immediately rebuilt and became "one of the finest law libraries in the country." Elliott Anthony's son Charles was 15 at the time of the fire.

primary_name: Bear Mountain State Park / Palisades Interstate Park
name: Bear Mountain State Park / Palisades Interstate Park
notes: Established 1909 through lobbying by a group led by Dr. Edward Lasell Partridge (X00643), husband of Gertrude Edwards Dwight Partridge (HD2001), 8th-generation Hooker descendant. After federal government rejected national park status, they secured New York State legislation establishing Bear Mountain State Park. Mary Harriman then bequeathed an additional 100,000 acres as a memorial to her husband E.H. Harriman. Partridge was appointed to the Palisades Interstate Park Commission in 1913 and served until 1930 — through three reappointments. During his tenure: Bear Mountain extended by ~50,000 acres; Storm King Highway built; Bear Mountain Bridge constructed.

name: Park Street Church, Boston
notes: Sereno Edwards Dwight (HD1970), son of Yale president Timothy Dwight IV (H00914), was ordained here Sep 3, 1817, and served as pastor 1817-1826. During his pastorate he greatly influenced young hymn writer Ray Palmer (1808-1887), author of "My Faith Looks Up to Thee." Tapping Reeve (X00614, founder of Litchfield Law School) is also connected: Dwight brought Lyman Beecher to Litchfield in 1810 to serve as Presbyterian minister, and Beecher later served at Park Street Church.

name: American Ethnological Society
notes: Founded 1842 in New York City. Theodore Dwight Jr. (HD2050) was a co-founder. The Society's founding mission was to document the languages, cultures, and histories of non-European peoples — with a particular focus, in Dwight's case, on demonstrating that African peoples had sophisticated literary and monotheistic cultures long before European colonization. The Society used this scholarship as an explicit tool of abolitionist argument. Dwight also worked through the Society to preserve and translate the Arabic-language autobiography of Omar ibn Said (1831), the only known slave narrative written in Arabic in the United States. (Wikipedia; flashbak.com 2019)

name: Wentworth Institute of Technology
notes: Founded 1904 from the $7M+ estate of Arioch Wentworth. Frederick William Atherton (HD2069) was one of three founding trustees. Opened to students 1911. (Wikipedia)

name: United States Military Academy (West Point)
notes: Founded 1802 at West Point NY. HOOKER TREE CONNECTION: Rev. George Washington Woodbridge (HD2163), son of General Jonathan Woodbridge (H00928), graduated West Point; served as 2nd Lieutenant US Army 1826-1829 before entering the Episcopal ministry. He became rector of Monumental Church Richmond VA 1843-1878 and escorted Flora Cooke Stuart to her dying husband Jeb Stuart in May 1864.

name: Jefferson Medical College, Philadelphia
notes: Founded 1824 Philadelphia PA (now Sidney Kimmel Medical College, Thomas Jefferson University). One of the leading medical schools in the US in the early 19th century. HOOKER TREE CONNECTION: Dr. Timothy Woodbridge (HD2172), first doctor born in Youngstown OH, graduated Jefferson Medical 1833. He served as Civil War surgeon at Johnson's Island POW camp and was appointed by President Hayes as surgeon at Fort Peck Montana 1879.

name: Vassar College
notes: Founded 1861 Poughkeepsie NY. One of the original Seven Sisters colleges. HOOKER TREE CONNECTION: Lucy Maria Edwards Remy (HD2224), daughter of John Stark Edwards (HD2183), granddaughter of Jonathan Edwards Jr. and great-great-great-granddaughter of Thomas Hooker, attended Vassar. Her obituary (Indianapolis newspaper, Dec 31, 1925) notes she attended the college and was "regarded as a woman of high intellectual attainments." Also: Louise Tayler-Jones (, Wellesley 1896 — though she is NOT a Hooker descendant, being from the second Tayler marriage).

primary_name: Radcliffe College (now Radcliffe Institute, Harvard University)
name: Radcliffe College (now Radcliffe Institute, Harvard University)
notes: Founded 1879, Cambridge MA. Women's coordinate college of Harvard. HOOKER TREE CONNECTION: Anne Frances Hazard Richardson (HD1587), daughter of Thomas Pierrepont Hazard (HD1579), graduated magna cum laude. She later won the Helen Homans Gilbert Award from Radcliffe for her work chairing Reading Is Fundamental 1981-1996. (HD1587 notes; Washington Post obituary Jul 28, 1999)

primary_name: University of Connecticut (founded 1881 as Storrs Agricultural School)
name: University of Connecticut (founded 1881 as Storrs Agricultural School)
notes:
Founded September 28, 1881 as the Storrs Agricultural School, Storrs, Tolland County, Connecticut. Named after brothers Charles and Augustus Storrs, who donated 170 acres of farmland, a former orphanage, $6,000, and several barns. Renamed: Storrs Agricultural College (1893, when women were officially admitted and it became Connecticut's land-grant college); Connecticut Agricultural College (1899); Connecticut State College (1933); University of Connecticut (1939). One of the top-ranked public universities in the United States. 
HOOKER TREE CONNECTION: Gov. Hobart Baldwin Bigelow (HD1438) — 50th Governor of Connecticut, 1881-1883 — signed the legislation accepting the Storrs gift and establishing the school. He was a 7th-generation descendant of Thomas Hooker through the Russell-Pierpont line: Thomas Hooker → Samuel Hooker → Mary Hooker Pierpont → Mary Pierpont Russell → Lt. Joseph Pierpont → Joseph Pierpont (Yale 1751) → Belinda Pierpont Bigelow → Gov. Hobart Baldwin Bigelow. (UConn official history; Wikipedia)

primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions (ABCFM)
name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions (ABCFM)
notes: Founded 1810 at Andover Seminary, Massachusetts by prominent Congregationalists. First American foreign missionary society; first missions to China and Ceylon. Yale President Rev. Timothy Dwight IV (H00914) was among the founding figures; Dwight Mission (established 1820 in present-day Arkansas/Oklahoma for the Cherokee) was named in his honor. The board operated Brainerd Mission (Chattanooga TN, est.1817) where Harriet Ellsworth Blunt (X02121) and Ainsworth Blunt (X02122) served the Cherokee. HOOKER TREE CONNECTIONS: 1. Rev. Timothy Dwight IV (H00914) — Yale President 1795-1817; founding ABCFM figure; Dwight Mission named for him. 5th-generation Hooker descendant. 2. Elias Cornelius (X00724) — ABCFM Secretary of Domestic Correspondence 1819-1832; conducted 1817 missionary tour to Creek and Cherokee; m. Mary Ann Hooker (H01355). 3. Rev. Edward William Hooker (H01354) — corporate ABCFM member from 1840. 4. Harriet Ellsworth Blunt (X02121) — served at Brainerd Mission 1821-1837; granddaughter of Rev. Cyprian Strong (H00299). 5. Ainsworth Blunt (X02122) — Brainerd missionary 1822-1837; accompanied Cherokee on Trail of Tears 1838; first mayor of Dalton GA. 6. Dr. Mary Floyd Cushman (HD2818, gen 8) — ABCFM medical missionary in Angola 1922-1953. 7. Samuel Gelston Dwight (HD2376) — ABCFM missionary to Hawaii; children named for ABCFM colleagues Juliette Cooke and Samuel Castle.

name: Baldwin Home Museum (Lahaina, Maui)
description: Residence of ABCFM missionary Dwight Baldwin 1836-1868 at Lahaina, Maui. Oldest standing house in Maui County. Deeded to Lahaina Restoration Foundation 1967. Named for Dwight Baldwin, whose first name likely honored Yale President Timothy Dwight IV.

name: Auburn Button Works (Auburn Plastics)
notes: Founded 1876 by John Hermon Woodruff (Yale 1863, X00821) as "Woodruff's Button Factory" in Auburn NY. Originally in the post office on Exchange Street; moved to 1900 Washington St in 1900; larger plant on Canoga Street 1911; second plant on 24 McMaster St WWII. Products: pearl and shellac buttons, typewriter keys, butt plates for rifles, 78-rpm records, molded plastic goods. Douglas Woodruff (HD2498, Yale 1905) succeeded as president until his death 1957. Renamed Auburn Plastics 1957 when button manufacturing ceased. Connected to Hooker tree through Caroline Porter Beardsley Woodruff (HD2488), 8th-generation descendant of Thomas Hooker through Susannah Edwards (H00382).

name: Yale Sheffield Scientific School
notes: Founded 1847 as the Yale Department of Philosophy and the Arts; became the Sheffield Scientific School 1861. Merged fully into Yale College 1956. Offered 3-year programs in engineering and applied science at lower cost than Yale College. Distinct from the "Yale" of liberal arts and law — Sheffield produced engineers, industrialists, and scientists. Notable Hooker-connected alumni: Joseph Wickham Roe (HD2556, 1895) — professor of industrial engineering, NYU; author of "English and American Tool Builders" (1916). Roe family papers at Yale: MS 774.

name: Connecticut Land Company
notes: Formed 1795 by 36 Connecticut investors who purchased the entire Connecticut Western Reserve from the State of Connecticut for over $1,000,000 — land the state had 'reserved' for citizens whose property was damaged by the British in the Revolution. In April 1807, Richfield Township was acquired for $26,087 by seven men: Col. Benjamin Tallmadge (H00757), Solomon Lathrop, Jeremiah Wilcox, Uriel Holmes Jr., Pierpont Edwards (H00386), Daniel Green, and Jonathan Dwight Sr. — two of the seven being Hooker descendants. Pierpont Edwards (H00386) invested $60,000 — approximately 5% of total company holdings. His son John Stark Edwards (HD2044) served as his first agent in the Reserve, but died Feb 22, 1813 in Warren, Trumbull County OH age 35 — the year Whittlesey rode through the black swamp and nearly died at Fort Meigs. Pierpont Township, Ashtabula County, Ohio is named for Edwards. In 1811, Heman Oviatt (husband of HD2934 Eunice Newton Oviatt) bought the northwest quarter of Richfield Township from Col. Tallmadge for $1.25 an acre. John Newton (HD2925) arrived in Richfield in the early 1820s and purchased most of the east center land, building the home later owned by Homer Carter. Elisha Whittlesey (X01997) arrived in Canfield June 27, 1806 — the same summer the company was active — and became public prosecutor for the entire Western Reserve. The company was ultimately a financial failure. (Richfield Township History; Ohio History Journal; Whittlesey Genealogy #310)

name: Savannah Educational Association (SEA)
notes: Founded by Savannah's African-American community after General Sherman captured Savannah (December 21, 1864) and the U.S. government confiscated the Montmollin Warehouse (23 Barnard Street (also referenced as 21 Barnard in some sources — likely a historical street renumbering; same building)). Established Bryan School at that location. Opening day January 10, 1865: hundreds of Black children marched from First African Baptist Church to the former slave pen building. July 1865: SEA schoolchildren publicly demonstrated their learning. Absorbed by the American Missionary Association 1867. (Georgia Historical Society marker, 23 Barnard Street (also referenced as 21 Barnard in some sources — likely a historical street renumbering; same building), Savannah)

name: Beach Institute
notes: Founded 1867 by the American Missionary Association, consolidating several Savannah schools including Bryan School (formerly in the Montmollin Warehouse, 23 Barnard Street — the building whose third floor had been a slave pen belonging to John Samuel de Montmollin II, grandson of abolitionist Jonathan Edwards Jr.). (Georgia Historical Society marker, 23 Barnard Street, Savannah)

name: First African Baptist Church
notes: Founded 1788 by Andrew Bryan — oldest Black Baptist church in America. On January 10, 1865, hundreds of Black children marched FROM this church TO the Montmollin Warehouse (former slave pen of John Samuel de Montmollin II) for the opening of Bryan School. (Georgia Historical Society marker, 23 Barnard Street, Savannah)

name: Williams College
notes: Private liberal arts college founded 1793 with funds from the estate of Ephraim Williams, a colonist killed in the French and Indian War (1755). One of the original "Little Three" liberal arts colleges.

name: Case Western Reserve University
notes: Founded 1826 in Hudson, Summit County, Ohio as Western Reserve College by a group of Connecticut Congregationalist settlers, with Rev. Giles Hooker Cowles (H00443) among its founders. Called 'the Yale of the West' — same motto (Lux et Veritas), same entrance standards, same curriculum as Yale. First college in northern Ohio. H00443 (Giles Hooker Cowles, gen 5 Hooker descendant) was named 'Hooker' for both his grandfather Giles Hooker and the Hooker ancestral line. He graduated Yale 1789, moved to Austinburg, Ashtabula County OH as a missionary (Ohio Home Missionary Society), and was instrumental in establishing the college infrastructure of the Western Reserve. First college west of the Appalachians to enroll (1832) and graduate (1836) an African-American student. Hudson was a major Underground Railroad stop. Co-founder Owen Brown was the father of abolitionist John Brown. The Newton family (HD2925 John Newton, HD2927 Eben Newton) and Elisha Whittlesey (X01997) all operated in the Western Reserve contemporaneously. Whittlesey was public prosecutor for the entire Western Reserve from 1806; Eben Newton was a judge and state senator. Moved to Cleveland 1882, merged with Case Institute of Technology 1967. H00443 is the brother of H00442 (Pamelia Cowles Thompson), whose granddaughter Laura Hooker Thompson Newton (HD0532) married John Newton (HD2925) — making the founding of Western Reserve College a family affair in the deepest sense. Among the original 12-member founding board of three presbyteries that met at Warren OH 1825 to choose a college site (selecting Hudson, now Summit County): from Grand River Presbytery, Rev. Dr. Giles Hooker Cowles (H00443) and Rev. Eliphalet Austin (X01418, with Harvey Coe and A. Griswold); from Portage Presbytery, Rev. Joseph Treat, John Steward, J. H. Whittlesey, Lemuel Porter; from Huron Presbytery, A. H. Betts, L. B. Sullivan, Hon. Samuel Cowles (of San Francisco — his namesake nephew HD3710 was 2 years old in 1825), D. Betts. Charter obtained 1826.

primary_name: Peabody Museum of Natural History, Yale University
name: Peabody Museum of Natural History, Yale University
notes: Endowed by George Peabody through his nephew Othniel Charles Marsh. Dana family connection: James Dwight Dana (X00899) — curator of mineralogy; Edward Salisbury Dana (X00898) — succeeded his father as curator 1874, trustee 1885-1929, chairman after Marsh's death 1899.

name: League of Universal Brotherhood
notes: Founded by Elihu Burritt (X01932) in 1846. International peace organization. name: Wadsworth Atheneum Museum of Art
notes: Founded 1842 by Daniel Wadsworth (HD1330); opened 1844. Oldest continuously operating public art museum in the United States. The building was designed to house Daniel's personal art collection and establish Hartford as a cultural center. Daniel was a patron of the Hudson River School — Thomas Cole and Frederic Church both exhibited here. The museum holds ART030: John Trumbull's 1784 portrait of Daniel (age 13) and his father Jeremiah (T00387) — painted 58 years before the museum existed, now hanging in the institution Daniel built. A portrait becomes its own home. Daniel Wadsworth descends from BOTH Thomas Hooker (through his mother H00362 Mehitable Russell → H00118 Mary Pierpont → H00034 Mary Hooker Pierpont) AND from John Talcott (through his father T00387 Jeremiah → T00068 Abigail Talcott → T00045 Gov. Joseph Talcott → T00035 Lt.-Col. John Talcott Jr.). Two founding Hartford lines united in one institution.

name: Troy Female Seminary (Emma Willard School)
notes:

Almira Hart Phelps (H00598) was vice-principal; wrote Lectures on Botany (1829) during this tenure — 10,000 copies sold in three years. Twelve or more Berlin CT girls attended on Willard's invitation and expense, including Jane Porter Hart (HD2963) and Harriet Hart (HD2964). As of 2026: endowment $119M; grades 9-12 plus postgraduate; #1 private school Upstate NY 2018.

name: Hartford Retreat for the Insane
notes:
Founded 1822, built 1823, opened to patients 1824. One of four such facilities in the nation at founding. First superintendent: Dr. Eli Todd (1823-1833) — pioneered "moral care" of the insane. T01062 Phineas Talcott Jr. served as steward 1828-1835; his wife I00197 Lora McLean was matron — patients called her "Good Mother Talcott." Boasted highest cure rate in the nation in its first decade. Grounds landscaped by Frederick Law Olmsted, 1860s. Became Institute of Living in 1943; incorporated into Hartford Hospital 1994. Now a comprehensive psychiatric facility at 200 Retreat Avenue, Hartford. Connecticut History Online article covers history in detail. [Apr 2026 cleanup — merged from duplicate INST108]: Chartered by Connecticut General Assembly 1822 following petition from the Connecticut State Medical Society; opened April 4, 1824. The only mental asylum in the United States founded by a state medical society. Eli Todd took up the cause after his sister's depression and suicide. Sources: APA Foundation; Goodheart 'Mad Yankees' (UMass Press, 2003); Braceland 'Institute of Living: The Hartford Retreat 1822-1972'; Connecticut History (CTHumanities).

name: Granite Mills

location.address: Just above Kelly Road, on the Hockanum River, Talcottville

notes:
Paper mill on the Hockanum River just above Kelly Road, Talcottville (Vernon) CT. GPS: 41.83210° N, 72.49648° W. Founded by T01052 Samuel Smith Talcott (1793-1882) as a linseed oil mill c.1838; converted to paper manufacturing after an 1863 fire. Manufactured albums, binders, boards, manilla and press papers — recycling old paper purchased in bales from New York, Boston, and Philadelphia. Destroyed by fire four times (1863, 1870, 1881, 1912) and rebuilt each time until the final 1912 fire. The 1881 fire article names owners as "Smith S. and Anson F. Talcott." Smith Stephen Talcott (1834-1888) — likely a son of T01052 Samuel — served as active agent from c.1864; he and T01052 ran the mill in parallel ("particularly confusing are the roles of the two S. S. Talcotts"). The Talcott Brothers (Charles Denison and Horace Wells) purchased the mill site in December 1912 primarily to acquire the water privilege. Despite the Talcott name, the Granite Mills Talcotts were from a different branch than the Talcottville mill owners. Site today: land between Tri-City Plaza and Hockanum River near Kelly Road; race exit from foundation preserved. Royal Talcott Journal, Nov 26, 1877: "young Nickols... got his foot caught in the belt" — earliest direct eyewitness account in the Royal Talcott Journal. SOURCE: Tankerhoosen Valley History, "Granite Mills (Circa 1850-1912)."
[former/alt detail] Also known as: Granite Paper Mills, Talcottville Paper Mill

primary_name: Talcott Street Congregational Church (now Faith Congregational Church)
name: Talcott Street Congregational Church (now Faith Congregational Church)
notes:
Founded 1826 by African Americans in Hartford after segregated treatment in white churches. Originally housed at Talcott Street, the congregation later took the name Faith Congregational Church. The church operated a school for African American children in its lower level — Hartford's regular school system technically accepted all children but African American parents established separate schools (1830) due to discriminatory treatment. The school met in the church's two lower rooms.

Rev. James W.C. Pennington pastored the congregation from 1840 to 1847 — writing during this period the first published history of African Americans (1841) and serving as a delegate to the Connecticut Anti-Slavery Society. It was here that John Hooker (H01626) became Pennington's friend and later, in 1851, legally purchased Pennington's freedom from his Maryland enslaver after the 1850 Fugitive Slave Law endangered him.

Other notable members of the period: Deacon James Mars (Connecticut Anti-Slavery Society executive committee); Henry Foster (state temperance society organizer); Ann Plato (teacher and author); Henry and Tamar Wilson (missionaries who accompanied the Mende return to Sierra Leone after the Amistad ruling).

name: Pacific-Union Club

location.address: 1000 California Street (Flood Mansion atop Nob Hill since 1912)

description: The Pacific-Union Club is San Francisco's oldest and most exclusive private men's club, formed in 1889 by the merger of the Pacific Club (1852) and the Union Club (1854) — both gold-rush-era gentlemen's clubs. Since 1912 it has occupied the James C. Flood Mansion atop Nob Hill at 1000 California Street, the only Nob Hill mansion to survive the 1906 earthquake and fire. Membership has historically included San Francisco's mercantile, banking, legal, and political elite. The Hooker family carried multi-generational membership: the 1905 Officers and Members directory lists three Hooker descendants — Charles Gay Hooker (H02703, address 967 Bush), C. Osgood Hooker (H04265, his son), and R.G. Hooker (Robert Gay Hooker, H04266, address 14 Post). A generation later, Charles Gay's grandson John Rodman Hooker (HD3243, Hooker & Fay senior partner) was also a member per his Golden Nugget Library biography. The Hooker presence at the club thus spans four generations — and is presumed to have continued with John Rodman's son Anthony Shreve 'Tony' Hooker.

description: Marlborough School is one of California's oldest private preparatory schools for girls, founded 1889 in Los Angeles by Mary Caswell. Marian Osgood Hooker (H04280, Class of 1894) studied art history directly under founder Mary Caswell, who likely inspired Marian's lifelong interest in travel and Italian architecture. Marian developed her photographic practice at Marlborough using a Kodak camera, printing her own glass plate negatives — assembling 'Marlborough Maidens,' a manuscript photo album with classmates posed as Greek goddesses, a Japanese orchestra, etc., now preserved at San Diego State University Special Collections.

name: University of California, Berkeley
description: The University of California's flagship Berkeley campus, founded 1868. Multiple Hooker descendants have UC Berkeley connections: Dr. Marian Osgood Hooker (H04280) earned her MD from UC Berkeley in 1910 and served as Assistant Medical Examiner at UC Berkeley from 1912. The Marian Osgood Hooker Collection at the UC Berkeley Environmental Design Archives contains 23 photograph albums of her European and California travels (1896-1926). name: The Thacher School
notes:
Founded 1889 by Sherman Day Thacher (I00592) on his 160-acre Casa de Piedra ranch outside Nordhoff (now Ojai) — began as informal tutoring of a single student. The oldest private boarding school west of the Mississippi. From founding day Sherman required every boy to care for a horse, famously saying "There's something about the outside of a horse that's good for the inside of a boy." Practically, students rode five miles into downtown Ojai daily just to collect the mail. Original complex destroyed by fire June 1895; rebuilt in redwood by Sherman's architect brother Edward. Incorporated as a nonprofit in 1924; Sherman continued as headmaster until his 1931 death. Succeeded by Morgan Barnes 1931-1936 (interim five years), then by Sherman's son Anson Stiles Thacher (HD4102) 1936-1949 — the last Thacher family member to head the school. Under Anson the student body expanded from Sherman's ~60-boy cap to 94 boys. Began admitting female students in 1977. Major periods of growth in the 1960s and mid-1970s reached 189 students by 1975. The 1999-2008 $82M capital campaign funded a performing arts center, student commons, and dormitories; the 2017-launched $160M campaign grew endowment from $125M to $171M by 2018, increased financial aid budget by 50.7% by 2020, and funded a new academic building, dining hall, faculty housing, and a solar facility supplying 90%+ of the school's energy. Horse program continues — freshmen ride and care for a horse during all athletic seasons; annual gymkhana event; Western riding required with English riding elective grades 10-12. Outdoors Program offers backpacking, rock climbing, cycling, sailing, horse camping, canyoneering, backcountry skiing, kayaking. The ranch-school model influenced a wave of similar schools across California, Arizona, New Mexico, and Wyoming in the early 20th century.

Author of six theological works during this pastorate including 'Outline of the Work of Grace' (1839), 'Influence of Physical Causes' (1846), 'Life of Ashbel Green VDM' (1849), 'Man Moral Physical' (1860), 'Autobiography of William Neill' as editor (1861), and 'The Brazen Serpent' (1864 — his final work).

The family Philadelphia Presbyterian succession runs from Sixth Presbyterian (1838-1861 under Joseph H. Jones DD) forward to Brown Memorial Baltimore (INST147, 1870+ under Joseph's nephew TD0240 John Sparhawk Jones) and later Calvary Presbyterian Philadelphia (John's subsequent pastorate).

name: University of the South (Sewanee)

founding_year.notes: Chartered 1858; cornerstone laid 1860; effectively re-founded after Civil War destruction.

notes:
Founded 1856-1860 by Episcopal Church Southern dioceses, with Bishop Leonidas Polk (X00867) as the principal founder and one of the original 9 trustees. Polk envisioned a Southern Episcopal university; he died June 1864 before the school opened. The original campus was destroyed during the Civil War; the university opened in 1868. Polk's grandson Frank Lyon Polk (HD5047) was awarded D.C.L. honorary degree from the University of the South in 1928.
Connected people roles: X00867 = Co-founder, principal organizing bishop, original trustee. HD5047 = Awarded D.C.L. honorary degree 1928 (grandson of co-founder).

name: American Baptist Home Mission Society
notes: Founded 1832 to support home (domestic) missionary work — distinguished from the older Foreign Mission boards. Edward Judson Brockett (HD1531) was an Executive Board member for many years and CHAIRMAN from June 1904 — a national-level lay leadership role per the 1905 Brockett Genealogy. ABHMS history includes founding schools and colleges in the Reconstruction-era South for newly emancipated African Americans, founding Native American missionary stations, and supporting Western frontier church-planting. INST creation note: A single Hooker-tree affiliation (Edward Judson Brockett) currently documented. Per v17 §0.4 the 2+ threshold is technically not met, but the role's significance (national chairman) and the Brockett-family Baptist network — including brother Dr. Linus Pierpont Brockett's documented Foreign-Missions specialty in Brooklyn — warrant creating the entry. Sam's directive in Stage 6Y-286.

name: Yale Law School
notes: One of the principal American law schools. Affiliated with Yale University, New Haven CT, since 1824. Among Hooker-tree affiliates: Ralph I. Ingersoll (1808), John Hooker, Louis H. Bristol (1859), John Wolcott Bristol (1879), Thomas Hooker Jr. (1906), Colin M. Ingersoll, George Pratt Ingersoll (LLB 1885).

name: Henry Hooker & Company
notes: New Haven carriage manufacturer, 1830-1920. Founded by Henry Hooker (H01452), who began in 1830 with a Southern repository for Northern carriages, then manufactured in New Haven as Hooker & Osborne, Hooker Candee & Co., and finally Henry Hooker & Co. (incorporated 1868). About 1862 the business moved into the former G. D. Cook Co. plant on State Street. One of the largest carriage works in the country, with a substantial export trade; later built automobile bodies, probably for Locomobile of Bridgeport. Officers c.1897: Edwin Marble (of New York) president; Frank H. Hooker (H02841) treasurer and manager; N. Albert Hooker (H02842) secretary.

name: Carrère and Hastings
notes: Beaux-Arts firm of John Merven Carrère and Thomas Hastings (Hooker spouse, I01429), 1885–1929. Designed the New York Public Library, the Frick mansion, the U.S. Senate/House office buildings, Woolsey Hall and the Hewitt Quadrangle at Yale (INST022), and — for easter-egg Henry Flagler (X03079) — the Ponce de Leon Hotel, Whitehall, and the Standard Oil Building.
